title: UK Inflation Jumps to 3.3% as Iran War Drives Fuel Price Surge, Pressuring Bank of England
detail: UK CPI rose to 3.3% in April 2026, driven by soaring fuel prices as the Iran war continued to disrupt global oil supply chains and feed through to consumer prices. The acceleration — coming just days after WTI crude rebounded to ~$95.75/bbl — adds significant pressure on the Bank of England, which faces the same stagflation dilemma confronting the Federal Reserve and ECB: inflation re-accelerating while growth risks mount. Fuel costs are the primary transmission channel: higher oil prices raised petrol prices and domestic energy costs, while supply-chain pass-through effects are broadening to food and goods. The Bank of England, which had been signaling a cautious easing bias, must now weigh whether to hold rates steady despite recession risk or tighten further to contain the second-order inflationary effects of the Iran war's energy shock. The UK figure aligns with the global pattern: euro-area HICP has also re-accelerated, US CPI (March) was 3.3%, and the IEA confirmed March 2026 saw the largest oil supply disruption in history at 10.1 mbpd. With the FOMC meeting April 28-29 and the ECB April 30, the Bank of England faces a pivotal window in which all major central banks must simultaneously navigate energy-driven inflation and slowing demand.

media.caption: UK inflation jumps to 3.3% as Iran war-driven fuel price surge feeds through to consumer prices

title: Sen. Tillis Blocks Kevin Warsh Fed Chair Confirmation Over DOJ-Powell Investigation; Warsh Vows Fed Independence, 'Regime Change'
detail: A day after Kevin Warsh's Senate Banking Committee confirmation hearing (April 21), CNN reported on April 22 that his path to Federal Reserve Chair is blocked by a critical Republican holdout: Sen. Thom Tillis (R-NC), who is refusing to support Warsh unless the Justice Department drops what he called a 'frivolous' investigation into outgoing Chair Jerome Powell. The Tillis holdout creates a significant confirmation obstacle with no clear resolution timeline — particularly urgent given Powell's term as Chair expires May 15, 2026. During his April 21 hearing, Warsh had testified he would NOT be President Trump's 'sock puppet,' pledging robust Fed independence and vowing to introduce 'regime change' at the Fed — including reducing the number of FOMC policy meetings per year and introducing a new inflation framework. Warsh is a known inflation hawk who has previously criticized quantitative easing and advocated for more aggressive balance sheet reduction. His confirmation would represent a significant policy shift at the Fed, coming as CPI stands at 3.3%, core PCE at 3.06%, and oil prices re-escalating. The Tillis blockage adds institutional uncertainty to an already fraught monetary policy outlook: who leads the FOMC at the April 28-29 meeting and beyond remains unresolved, layering political risk on top of the existing stagflation bind. Markets watched for any signals about whether the White House would pressure Tillis, what concessions might break the deadlock, or whether Trump might consider an alternative nominee.

media.caption: Sen. Tillis blocks Kevin Warsh Fed Chair confirmation over DOJ investigation into Powell; Warsh pledged independence and 'regime change' at the Fed
